data.title: Fed's Miran is 'Unconcerned about Inflation,' Urges Rate Cuts
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Stephen Miran reiterated his stance on lowering interest rates, arguing that a strong jobs report for January doesn't weaken the case for easing monetary policy while inflation remains under control.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

"As long as I remain unconcerned about inflation, I think it makes sense to continue trying to underwrite the labor market with looser monetary policy, particularly as supply expands more than demand and the economy can grow without generating inflation," Miran said at a Dallas Fed event.

Inflation Slows Ahead of March FOMC Meeting

Miran's statement was put to the test when the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) published January's Consumer Price Index (CPI) this morning, showing inflation rising by 2.4% annually. That marked the lowest rate of growth since May 2025. In addition, core CPI, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, increased by 2.5% from the prior year, registering its lowest level since March 2021.

Miran has voted in favor of rate cuts in every Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting since he joined the board in September. The next meeting is scheduled for March 18.

data.author: Eddie Pan

Despite these concerns, several data center stocks still boast both a Strong Buy and impressive upside from Wall Street analysts. Using the TipRanks Compare Top Data Center Stocks tool, this article identifies three such stocks: Oracle (ORCL), MongoDB (MDB), and SBA Communications (SBAC).

Oracle (ORCL)

data.title: Dow Jones Today: DJIA Rallies on Cooling Inflation as Pentagon Withdraws Updated 1260H List
data.description:
The Dow Jones (DJIA) is trading higher on Friday following an encouraging inflation reading from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS).

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) for January rose by 0.2% month-over-month, below the consensus estimate of 0.3%. It increased by 2.4% on an annual basis, below the expectation of 2.5% and registering the slowest pace of growth since May 2025. Core CPI, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, increased by 0.3% from the prior month and 2.5% annually, both of which were in line with their estimates.

"Headline CPI inflation was a touch softer than expected in January, delivering a welcome surprise to the downside at the beginning of the year," wrote Oxford Economics lead economist Bernard Yaros in a note.

This morning, the Pentagon published a document detailing the inclusion of Alibaba (BABA), Baidu (BIDU), Nio (NIO), and several other companies to the Section 1260H list. The list flags Chinese companies the Pentagon views as having ties to the country's military. However, the document has since been taken down from the Pentagon's website, with some attributing the move to the U.S. seeking to ease tensions before the Trump-Xi summit in April.

Elsewhere, the Financial Times reported that the Trump administration is planning to exempt some steel and aluminum products from tariffs in light of affordability concerns, which have affected products like drink cans and pie tins. Since the tariffs were imposed last June, the price of aluminum has risen by 24%, while steel has edged higher by about 4%. However, White House trade adviser Peter Navarro later denied the report, saying that Trump will maintain a "no exemptions, no exclusions" approach.

Earlier this week, a study from the New York Fed found that Americans absorb 90% of the increased costs from tariffs. The average tariff rate on U.S. imports has risen to 13% from 2.6%, with importers passing on higher costs to consumers.

The Dow Jones is up by 0.50% at the time of writing.
data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: U.S. Removes List Linking Alibaba, Baidu to China Military
data.description:
This morning, the Pentagon added several Chinese companies to its Section 1260H list, which identifies firms it says are linked to or supportive of China's military. The companies included Alibaba (BABA), Baidu (BIDU), and BYD (BYDDY).

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

However, the document showing the new additions has since been removed from the Pentagon's website. In its place is a message stating, "This Document Was Withdrawn."

Alibaba Challenges Addition to Section 1260H List

It's unclear why the document was taken down, although some experts speculate that President Trump didn't want to raise tensions ahead of his April summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, which could complicate trade or security negotiations.

"Alibaba is not a Chinese military company nor part of any military-civil fusion strategy," said a company spokesperson in response to its addition to the list. "We will take all available legal action against attempts to misrepresent our company."

Companies that were already on the list include Tencent (TCEHY), CATL (CYATY), and DJI.

data.title: A Home Depot With Live DJ? Home Depot Stock (NYSE:HD) Notches Up as QXO Gains Ground
data.description:
While home improvement giant Home Depot (HD) has been the scene of a lot of noteworthy events of late, from Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) raids to protests of various stripes, one potentially even more noteworthy event hit a Home Depot in San Antonio: a live DJ set. File it under "strange but true," this event caught attention and even sparked some concern. But Home Depot investors rolled with the punch, and sent shares up fractionally in Friday morning's trading.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Caught by another DJ out in the field, video emerged of a second, different DJ who got on top of a pile of wood planks, set up a turntable on a tool storage chest, and began to play the 1996 track Escandalo by La Sonora Dinamita. Comments ranged from plans to turn the power tool section into a rave to some believing it was the "…funniest thing I ever saw that day."

Others, however, believed that the music might well lure in the kind of people who would find themselves targeted by ICE raids. That prompted some to wonder if this was a deliberately-staged trap. But Home Depot stuck to its guns, insisting that it was not coordinating with ICE or Border Patrol, and that it was legally constrained from interfering in their operations in any way.

Gadget Haven

Beyond the sudden DJ appearances, Home Depot also rolled out some new gadgets that were not specifically power tools, but would likely be welcome in some situations. This is a solid development; while Home Depot concentrates heavily on home improvement, sometimes improving the home is as easy as adding a new feature.

For instance, it brought out a new battery-operated outdoor security camera from Arlo, as well as the XGIMI MoGo DLP portable projector for those considering some outdoor movies this summer. Finally, Ryobi brought out a new power source / charger system for those battery-driven tools. Not only will it charge batteries, but it can also use those batteries as a power source for, for example, that aforementioned projector.

Is Home Depot a Good Long-Term Buy?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Moderate Buy consensus rating on HD stock based on 16 Buys, five Holds and one Sell assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. After a 4.71% loss in its share price over the past year, the average HD price target of $410.22 per share implies 4.26% upside potential.
data.author: Steve Anderson

data.title: U.S. Officials Warn "Cupid Doesn't Ask for Crypto" in New Valentine's Day Fraud Alert
data.description:
U.S. attorneys are speaking out to make sure your search for love doesn't end in financial ruin. The U.S. Attorney's Office for the Northern District of Ohio just launched a campaign called "Cupid Doesn't Ask for Crypto" to highlight a major rise in holiday fraud. They warn that scammers are currently flooding dating apps and social media with fake profiles, hoping to find people who are lonely or looking for a connection during Valentine's week. These criminals are pros at moving quickly from hello to asking for your life savings.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

How the Scammers "Fatten Up" their Targets

Officials say the most common threat right now is a long-term fraud called "pig butchering." The name comes from how the criminals work: they find a victim and "fatten them up" by sending constant messages of love and affection for weeks or months. Once they have built total trust, they casually mention a great way to make money through a crypto trading app. The victim is then coached on how to move their money into a fake platform that looks real but is actually just a trap designed to steal every dollar.

Red Flags to Watch Out For

U.S. prosecutors and the FBI say you should be extremely careful if your new online interest does any of the following:

  • They shower you with love bombing and talk about a future together almost immediately.
  • They always have a convenient excuse for why they cannot do a video call or meet in person.
  • They push you to move the conversation from the dating app to an encrypted service like Meta's WhatsApp (META) or Telegram.
  • They claim to be an expert in crypto and offer to help you trade or invest.

The Damage from These Scams Is Huge

The damage from these scams is massive. In just one recent case in Ohio, a woman lost her entire life savings after being tricked into a fake investment site. Prosecutors also recently charged a man who allegedly stole over $8 million from seniors using these same romantic traps. These aren't just small-time crooks; they are often part of large crime rings in Southeast Asia that use trafficked workers to run hundreds of scams at once from specialized compounds.

Key Takeaway

Let's be honest; a real romantic partner is never going to ask you to wire them crypto or join a secret trading platform. These scammers are incredibly good at what they do, and they know exactly how to play on your emotions. Federal agents have seen everyone from young professionals to retirees lose everything because they trusted the person on the other side of the screen. If you feel pressured to move money for someone you have never actually met in person, the best gift you can give yourself this year is to hit the block button.

Investors can track the prices of their favorite cryptos on the TipRanks Cryptocurrency Center. Click on the image below to find out more.
data.author: Annika Masrani

data.title: SNDK or QCOM: Morgan Stanley Picks One Top AI Semiconductor Stock to Buy
data.description:
AI adoption is accelerating across data centers, cloud platforms, and enterprise systems, but one part of the hardware stack is becoming a real constraint – memory chips. Advanced AI workloads depend heavily on high-performance memory to store, move, and process enormous datasets at high speed. Training and running large models is not just about compute anymore; it is equally about how fast memory is available and how efficiently it can be accessed.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That demand is now rising faster than supply. High-bandwidth and advanced DRAM production requires specialized manufacturing capacity, long build-out cycles, and complex packaging, which limits how quickly output can expand. The result is a tightening memory market and upward pricing pressure in certain segments.

These constraints do not affect every part of the tech sector equally. Hardware makers that rely on memory components – including PC and smartphone vendors – can face higher input costs and potential supply bottlenecks. By contrast, companies directly focused on memory production or closely tied to high-end memory demand may benefit from stronger pricing power and improved margins. Other semiconductor firms sit somewhere in between, depending on their product mix and customer exposure.

Against this backdrop, Morgan Stanley analyst Joseph Moore, who ranks among the top 2% of analysts on TipRanks, draws a clear distinction between companies leveraged to memory trends and those more exposed to end-device demand. Applying that framework to SanDisk (NASDAQ:SNDK) and Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM), Moore comes down in favor of only one, while taking a more cautious stance on the other. His reasoning, and what it implies for investors, is worth a closer look.

SanDisk

Let's start with SanDisk. This company is probably familiar to you – especially if you make regular use of portable USB drives. SanDisk is a leader in that market; its handy little thumb drives, with the company logo on them, are easy to find and convenient to use to keep files and information portable. SanDisk has been in operation since 1988, was traded on Wall Street from 1995 to 2016, and was owned by Western Digital from 2016 until 2025. It spun off from Western Digital last February and now trades on the NASDAQ index.

SanDisk's most public face might be thumb drives, but the company is well-known as an important supplier of higher-end memory, too. The company sells both internal and external solid-state drives (SSDs) and offers data storage solutions based on NAND flash technology. These solutions have found favor in data center applications and in edge devices. SanDisk operates globally, boasting more than 10,000 employees and a footprint in more than 30 countries. The company is proactive about protecting its intellectual property rights and holds over 11,000 patents worldwide.

In recent months, SanDisk's strong position as a high-end memory provider has provided powerful support for the stock. Shares in SNDK are up over 1,600% in the past 12 months and 155% just year-to-date.

These gains have been driven by data center demand, which, in fiscal period 2Q26, the last reported, was up 64% sequentially and 76% year-over-year. The data center segment brought in $440 million in revenue for SanDisk during fiscal 2Q.

The company reported strong results in other parts of that report, with total revenue at $3.3 billion, up 61% year-over-year and coming in $337 million better than had been anticipated. SanDisk's $6.20 non-GAAP EPS popped an impressive 404% year-over-year and beat the forecast by $2.66 per share. SanDisk ended the fiscal quarter with $1.54 billion available in cash and liquid assets.

In his coverage of SanDisk, Morgan Stanley analyst Joseph Moore takes a constructive view of SanDisk and argues that the company is operating from a position of real strength rather than a short-lived upswing.

"Earnings are above the long term trend, but based on our data points it seems likely to stay that way for more than one year – really for as long as the AI trajectory remains this robust. Enterprise NAND demand is every bit as inelastic as DRAM, and while there are likely to be some headwinds in consumer because of price increases, the supply picture is also really positive – capital spending remains at low levels, amid reports that some NAND cleanroom space may even be allocated to DRAM. That points to a long runway for pricing increases and for EPS revisions," Moore noted.

The top-rated analyst goes on to say what this means for investors, in quantitative terms, writing: "At what is still a single-digit P/E multiple on peak earnings power potential—and roughly a 9x multiple on our new CY26 EPS we think there's more upside here as long as the data-center build continues unabated. And while earnings are above our view of long term earnings power, which is our basis for valuation, the level of peak earnings is a major part of the through cycle formula – and they get to keep the cash flow they generate along the way. With earnings crossing the $80 run rate, and no end in sight in the intermediate term, we are moving our through cycle earnings power estimate to $30."

Moore finishes by rating SNDK as Overweight (i.e., Buy), with a $690 price target that implies an upside of 13% for the next 12 months. (To watch Moore's track record, click here)

Overall, SanDisk holds a Moderate Buy consensus rating on the Street, based on 15 recent analyst reviews that break down to 11 Buys and 4 Holds. (See SNDK stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Rivian Stock (RIVN) Skyrockets as Five-Star Analysts Weigh in on Q4 Results
data.description:
EV maker Rivian (RIVN) is surging in today's trading following its fourth-quarter earnings results. Interestingly, Automotive revenue and gross profit were still under pressure, mainly because deliveries declined following changes to U.S. EV incentives and because average selling prices were lower. However, the bigger picture showed progress. In 2025, Rivian generated about $144 million in gross profit compared to a $1.2 billion loss in 2024. This sharp improvement was driven largely by fast growth in its higher-margin software and services segment, along with better profitability on each vehicle sold.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Looking ahead to 2026, the company expects deliveries to increase to a range of 62,000 to 67,000 vehicles. Management described 2026 as a year that will be focused on investing and scaling production as it prepares to ramp up its R2 and midsize platforms. As a result, Analyst reactions were mixed but leaned slightly optimistic. For instance, Wedbush kept its Buy rating and $25 price target, with five-star analyst Dan Ives saying he remains confident in Rivian's long-term strategy as it optimizes R1 production and prepares for its next growth phase.

Separately, Needham called 2026 a transitional year. It noted that while higher spending on autonomy research and new launches is weighing on near-term results, improving vehicle economics and strong software growth tied to the Volkswagen (VWAGY) joint venture support future margin expansion. On the other hand, Mizuho, led by five-star-rated Vijay Rakesh, maintained its Sell rating by arguing that the broader EV sector continues to face significant challenges.

Is RIVN Stock a Buy or Sell?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Hold consensus rating on RIVN stock based on nine Buys, eight Holds, and four Sells assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. Furthermore, the average RIVN price target of $18.14 per share implies 6.6% upside potential.
data.author: Vince Condarcuri
